speaker: interviewer
text: Rolling.

speaker: interviewer
text: Today's date is May 19th, 1995. We are conducting an interview with Sophie Waters in Highland Park, Illinois, USA. The interview is being conducted in English.

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: Just different electronics.

speaker: interviewee
text: Sure.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: We're rolling.

speaker: interviewer
text: You're rolling?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah, if you want to go ahead and--

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm Doris Lazarus. Today's date is May 19th, 1995. I am conducting an interview with Sophie Waters in Highland Park, Illinois, USA.

speaker: interviewer
text: We're rolling.

speaker: interviewer
text: Please state your name and spell it.

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is Sophie Waters. S-O-P-H-I-E W-A-T-E-R-S.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your name at birth, please.

speaker: interviewee
text: My name at birth was Sophie Keizer, K-E-I-Z-E-R.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the date of your birth?

speaker: interviewee
text: The 9th of October, 1907.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you are how old today?

speaker: interviewee
text: I am now 87 years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the city and country?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born in London, England.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what kind of area did you live in? Was it a Jewish neighborhood?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was on the East side of London, that I do know.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of neighborhood was it?

speaker: interviewee
text: A middle class-- middle class neighborhood.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you lived there with your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: My parents lived there, and I was the second oldest child.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you tell us your parents' names and your brothers' and sisters' names?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. My-- well, they came much later, of course. My parents-- my mother was Dinah Keizer, but her maiden name was Roselaar. And my father was Samuel Keizer.

speaker: interviewer
text: And brothers and sisters?

speaker: interviewee
text: And then I got-- after I was born, I had another sister, Janie, and then a brother, Nathan Leon. Then a brother, Phillip, and then a sister, Margaret. And that was six children in one family.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what about your early home life. You attended school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I attended school. And I know I was 11 or 12 and I had to go to a high school. And I had to do an exam for that, from regular public school. And I-- the teachers said I was pretty good in school, so I could do that even when I was 11. But I got influenza at that time. It was quite normal that most-- at that time they didn't have what they have today, all these-- that takes care of it. So I could not go the first time. And when I was 12, I was sick again. But I went out of bed and I went and done my exam. And normally, you win a prize money. But I didn't come in first, so I did not get the money. But I could go to school for free. And that was my experience as a child.

speaker: interviewer
text: And my mother came home and she said, how dare you go out of bed? You're not well. And I said, well, I wanted to do the exam. I wanted to get through with that. And then I went to high school. Was this a Jewish school or a public school?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was-- no, a public school. And I had to walk a long way-- a very long way. And it was mixed. It was Jewish and Gentile. And it was very good-- a very good school. Because I remember, we learned German there, which was quite something that, in those years, they hardly ever did. So it was quite a decent school. But we had to wear special clothing-- a jumper with a blouse and a straw hat, and it had the emblem in front with the-- the name of the school. And I know the boys always used to try and beat us up and break those straw hats. But then I was about 16, 16 and a half maybe, and we emigrated through the Netherlands, Holland.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, but while you were still in London--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --and you were attending the school, how were the relations between the Jewish students and the non-Jewish students?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very good. We never had any troubles. Never, never, never.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you have--

speaker: interviewee
text: There were never, never. No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you aware of any antisemitism at all in London?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing. No way at all. No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did--

speaker: interviewee
text: We never knew of that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your family belong to a synagogue?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes. My father was-- in Holl-- in England, he was the shamus of the shul, because it was in our street. And of course, we went to-- to see my dad in shul and-- oh yes, we-- we were really raised Jewish. And-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] but not kosher. But we were very Jewish. We never had anything in the house that was not appropriate for us to eat.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you celebrate Shabbat?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yes, we always had Shabbat. And Friday night, definitely. And my friends were Jewish.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you celebrate other Jewish holidays?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. We are celebrated all the Jewish holidays. And that was a must, because my parents were raised pretty Jewish.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did your father do?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father was first a shoe repairer, and then he went into business. And he let the shoes-- [LAUGHS] he got-- [BACKGROUND NOISE] he said to my mother, forget about the shoes. I'm going into business. And she said, the customers will come and they will expect the shoes to be repaired. He said, well, if they don't want to wait, just give them back not repaired. And if they want to wait, when I have time, I will repair them. In the meantime, he became quite-- he had a very good brain.

speaker: interviewer
text: He had his schooling in Holland. So he was, of course, a Dutch person. And he-- the only time we had a very bad experience was, I was in the street car with my mother and father. And there was a man and he says, how come you are not in the Army? Because that was in the Army of 1914, First World War I.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he said-- my father said, because I had rheumatic fever, which was true. As a younger man, he had rheumatic fever. So he wasn't allowed to go into the Army. Yes, you people always know how to get out of things. That was the only time that we ever had heard antisemitism. The only time. But never again.

speaker: interviewer
text: When your father decided to move to family to Holland, what were his reasons for leaving England?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the reason was he wanted us-- because the girls were the oldest in the family. And he wanted us to have a Dutch-- from his side, a Dutch person as a husband. Because it so happened we had a neighbor, and my sister was a little older. She was a very pretty girl.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the neighbor came in, and he said, I hear you're-- was Jewish. You're dating my son. So she said, I only went out once with him to a movie. That's not dating. And he said, well, how much [? nadan ?] are you bringing along? We never knew the word or knew what that meant, because that was something very different than we had ever experienced. And my sister said, what do you mean by that? He said, how much money are you bringing? She said, when I marry, I bring myself and that's enough. And you know what? When we had the carpenter, he made an opening, and that's called a door. You better get out before I do something that you would not like. And that was the end of going out with the boys in England. So your father decided to move to Holland.

speaker: interviewee
text: So then father said, I think we should go to Holland and you'll find nice boys there. And that's what we did.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how old were you at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know exactly. Between 16 and 17. I know that. And I never had any schooling in Holland, and neither did my sister, my older sister. We both never went to school. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you live in Holland, what city?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Amsterdam.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the address of your home?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. [DUTCH] 101. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] [? Amsterdam ?] right in the city. We opened a business, and we were working in the business for my dad. And then my sister met this young man, and then his brother met me, later. And, well, they happened to be twins. And that's how we married. We had beautiful weddings. Of course, we had the real Jewish wedding, with the-- the coal, the-- the seven berakhots, and everything the way it should be done.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year was that in?

speaker: interviewee
text: I got married in 1935.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what were the conditions in Holland at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very good. I got an apartment, and I had it installed beautiful-- with beautiful furniture. Had it all custom made and nice window curtains. And I-- really, a beaut-- had the most beautiful furniture. Lovely living room and dining room with the green-- I remember green velour upholstered. I had gray carpeting, and gray, velour drapes, because that was the fashion at that time-- velour. And as a matter of fact, I still have my silver from then, and that is more than 60 years old. My silver is at least 65 years old. And I still have most of it. And-- and also, a gift I have, that's upstairs, Chinese-- 12 cups and saucers and tea pots and milk and sugar dish, and I gave that to my daughter. And-- but we lived very nice, and we never knew of anything. There was never any hate, and nobody asked you what you were, which was very strange, coming to America.

speaker: interviewer
text: When we first came to America, and I worked in a store on South Michigan Avenue in a dress shop, and nobody every asked what you were or what you are. But when we opened our own store in Joliet, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] people came in and said, what are you? I said, what-- what do you mean by that? Because I had never heard of it. What is your religion? And I said, well, religion-- we are Jewish. Born Jewish. Oh. And that was the very first time I had heard anybody ask that question. But that was here in this country, after the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was in-- in America.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now back in Holland, what year was it that you first sensed any danger?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my daughter was born in 1936. And in 1940, the Germans invaded Holland. That was in May, 1940. I know the soldiers were there, but they overran them in no time. And they had the Germans for the boss. And then in 1942, they took our store.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let me ask you-- back in 1940, now the Germans occupied Holland.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And were you fearful?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I-- we never knew about anything, because there was never the hate in Holland. We never knew of antisemitism in Holland. Never. And we didn't know what was going on.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any idea what was going on in Europe?

speaker: interviewee
text: No idea at all. No idea. The first time I can remember, my-- my daughter went to kindergarten. And I had a friend, and her son went to kindergarten. And she said, what are you going to do? I said, I don't know what you mean by what are you going to do?

speaker: interviewer
text: She said, well, would you like to see your daughter eat from the garbage cans, if they send you elsewhere? I said, well, nobody would do that. I-- I never thought that that would exist, even. So we didn't know about it till they picked us up, and we were-- So from 1940 to 1942--

speaker: interviewee
text: We were--

speaker: interviewer
text: --you were in your own home--

speaker: interviewee
text: In my own home.

speaker: interviewer
text: --And felt relatively safe.

speaker: interviewee
text: Very safe. We weren't allowed out in the street after 8:00. But I had neighbors that used to come over on the QT, you know, and be with us. I just said, be very careful, because we can get caught. But that never happened, because we were not afraid, actually.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was that, that you were told not to be outside after a certain time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Right away. They made-- they-- they declared that the Jews were not allowed out after 8:00, at evening, in the street. And then they started with us that we had to wear a star. So we always put the star on-- we were in a store, and we wore a special coat in the store.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I always had one sewn on my coat. It had to be sewn on. It could not be pinned. Had to be sewn on. And one morning, I was still in my apartment, and my sister called me. She said, your husband was taken away.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because they came by and my husband was cleaning the window-- the outside window-- to make it look nice and clean. And he forgot to put his coat on with the star. And because he did not have the star on, they picked him up.

speaker: interviewer
text: This was in what year?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was in '42. And my sister was also in the store-- my young sister that came after to me, Janie. And she went up to these people, and she said, don't take my brother-in-law. He has a little girl. She's only-- she was six. She was born in '36, and this was '42. She's only six.

speaker: interviewer
text: What would she do without her father, you know, that you are taking? Oh, they said-- she said, you-- you can help us. And she said, in what way? They said if you would tell those people-- Jews-- that are in hiding.

speaker: interviewee
text: And she said, I'm sorry. But I am not that kind of a person. I wasn't-- I sucked my mother's breast and there was no hate in the milk. And that I would never do. You-- I'd rather you'd take my tongue out now before I can say another word. But I would never, never do that. And they looked at her and they-- they thought she was quite a nice person to answer that way. So they took my husband to a place where they nearly killed them. And from there, they sent them to Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: So then he said to my sister, you will see. I will bring him back by 1:00. He will be back. This happened at 8:00 or 8:30 in the morning. So [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] indeed, they came back with my husband, and brought him back. And they said, now, always wear your jacket-- coat with the star.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he said, well, it was just a misfortune that I did not put it on early in the morning. So anyway, they brought him back. But my sister had no peace from this one man. He always wanted to touch her breasts, and she thought, well, if he touches my breast, I won't get sick from that.

speaker: interviewer
text: And-- but she was married, and she told her husband. So he said, where do you live? And she told him where she lived, and indeed, he used to come up and visit her. He thought he would get more done from her. But that was out of the question. Who-- who was this man?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was a Gentile. He was a Nazi. He worked for the Germans. He was a Dutchman, but he worked for the Germans. And-- but he had a lot of respect for my sister, anyway.

speaker: interviewer
text: He sometimes brought her a chicken or something to eat, and she thanked him. And she said, see? You are a good person after all. And she had to, you know, be nice to him. And then we-- I wanted to know, did your husband tell you what took place when he was arrested?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, he heard, because he was in a cell, but he heard screaming in this place. It happened in the [NON-ENGLISH] that's further than where we had the store. He said, I had never heard such screams in my life. And they did not mishandle him. But the way they mishandled the people there was unbelievable. And my husband told me that. He said, Sophie, the people were screaming for their life. But they were-- after that, they took them to Auschwitz. And that's where my husband would have eventually ended up. But he came home, thank God. And he was very careful always to wear the star.

speaker: interviewer
text: What else did you notice that changed in 1942?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, in 1942, we were taken, at that time, in the-- on the 25th of September, they came at night with a paper for myself, my husband, and my child, to go to where the Germans were. And then we would go to a camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I asked them very nicely, I just put my child to bed. I worked very hard today in the store. It happened to be very busy. And my husband and I are really very tired, and my child just went to sleep. Will you come back tomorrow? Because we-- that same day, my dad had given the Germans a lot of money to get what they call a [GERMAN]. That was the name that meant a paper that you would not be picked up, and you would go to Portugal. And they said, who do you think you are, that you can order us around?

speaker: interviewee
text: And I said, no, I'm not ordering you. I'm asking you to come back tomorrow. Because then I knew I would have the paper in my hand. And they said, no, you have to come with us. So right away, we had to go in-- in a wagon. And they took us to a pace in Amsterdam. And from there, they put you on the train to Westerbork, to the camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they tell you why they were taking you or where they were taking you?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, not a word. Nothing is said. Because you're Jewish, of course.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you able to take any belongings from your home?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I-- I never thought of that, so I had nothing ready at that time. Because I was one of the first that-- I read the camp opened the 1st of July in '42, and this was the 25th of September in '42. And I didn't even know what was going on.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have a chance to say goodbye to any of your other family?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. The only thing I could do was-- a neighbor was standing outside. I handed them the keys. I said, give this to my parents, because they lived opposite us. And that's all. And then we went. And they took us first to a holding place, where the Germans were the boss. And they called out names of people with that could-- that they needed in Amsterdam to do things for them. So I said to my husband, whatever name they call, we'll do like it's ours and we'll walk out. My husband said, I think you're crazy all of a sudden. I said, no. I'll hold our child, and you hold onto me, and we're just going to walk out. But that night, the Germans were so drunk, they didn't call one name-- not one.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where were you being held at this point?

speaker: interviewee
text: So we were held in this place, in the [NON-ENGLISH]. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] then we had go by train to-- now I forgot the name of the place. And I knew it, really, because it was not right to the camp. The train did not go to the camp. It went-- it's at least an hour walk after the train. We had to walk to the camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long were you on a train?

speaker: interviewee
text: I wouldn't remember that anymore. Couple of hours. And then we were put into a camp, my husband separate from us. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: How were you initially treated when you arrived at the camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: We-- we had to give our name. The Jewish people were taking the names from the Jewish Institute actually. And they were putting the names down of everybody that arrived, because they kept, actually, records of everybody.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they put our name down. And then I looked up, and there I see a German SS. And it happened to be a young man standing behind, and he motioned me to come over to him. And he happened to be a friend of my brother, not knowing that he was with the SS. We knew he was from German descent, but not knowing that he was that. So I went over to him, and he said, do like you don't know me, but I'm going to your parents' tonight. I will keep you here, that you will not go on the transport tomorrow. Because the next day was already a transport. And every-- twice a week, they sent a thousand people away, twice a week. So he said, I will keep you. People that were with us said, why don't you take your lipstick and put red spots on your door, so then they'll think she has chickenpox or some sickness? And I said, no, I'm not going to do that. I'm just going to take it, and just what comes will come. And I did not tell-- I wasn't allowed to tell anybody that I knew him. And so, when I came back, and they were writing our names down, they said, do you know who you were speaking to? I said, no. Don't forget we traveled the whole night and I'm really a little tired.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they said, well, he's one of the big shots here in the camp. I said, oh, I'm sorry. But I don't-- I didn't know who he was. Because I was not allowed to tell anybody that I knew him. So then when our name came, I wrote every day, and you could go to one of the offices and get a card or letter to family. And every day, I went there and I wrote to my parents-- my father, especially, get me out of this place. Because you put a lot of money into it, and we are still here. And that--
